subject: Endocrinology
title: DKA vs HHS without the confusion
hook: Two emergencies, one comparison you must know cold. Swipe.

points.heading: Who and how fast
points.body: DKA: younger, type 1 diabetes, onset over hours. HHS: older, type 2 diabetes, onset over days with marked dehydration.

points.heading: Glucose and ketones
points.body: DKA: raised glucose with high ketones, pH below 7.3 and low bicarbonate. HHS: very high glucose (often above 30 mmol/L) with minimal ketones and near-normal pH.

points.heading: Osmolality
points.body: HHS is defined by a very high serum osmolality (above 320), which drives the hyperosmolar state. In DKA the osmolality is usually less extreme.

points.heading: Management priorities
points.body: Both need fluids first. In DKA, start a fixed-rate insulin infusion early. In HHS, fluids alone usually lower the glucose, so add insulin only if there is significant ketonaemia or once glucose stops falling. HHS also needs VTE prophylaxis; in DKA watch for cerebral oedema.

caption: DKA and HHS get confused under pressure, so lock in the differences now. DKA is a ketoacidosis in younger type 1 patients over hours; HHS is a hyperosmolar state in older type 2 patients over days. Fluids come first in both, but HHS is driven by fluids rather than early insulin. Save this before your next on-call.
